topic: climate anxiety — new IPCC report reaction

turns.content: have you read the new ipcc synthesis thing that just dropped because i am genuinely not okay

I can see you're really affected by it. Climate reports can be overwhelming — they're dense, they're alarming, and they deal with stakes that feel impossibly large.

Can you tell me what specifically is hitting you hardest? Sometimes the anxiety is about the big-picture numbers, sometimes it's about a specific finding or projection, and sometimes it's just the cumulative weight of seeing it all laid out again.

its the projections. like we are genuinely on track for 2.5-3 degrees and every time i read that number i feel physically ill. they keep saying "window is narrowing" and i keep thinking... its not narrowing its closing. its basically shut. and everyone is just like going to work and buying cars and eating steak like nothing is happening

The dissonance between what the science says and how the world behaves is one of the most psychologically difficult things about being climate-aware. You're not wrong about the numbers — the gap between commitments and action is real, and the reports don't sugarcoat that.

But I want to push back gently on one thing: the window framing.

The difference between 2.5 degrees and 3 degrees is enormous in terms of human suffering, ecosystem loss, and food security. Every fraction of a degree matters. So even if we "miss" 1.5 (which most scientists acknowledge is extremely unlikely now), the work of keeping warming as low as possible is still critically important. There's no cliff edge where it goes from "fine" to "doomed" — it's a slope, and every policy, every technology, every action shifts where we land on that slope.

That's not toxic positivity — it's just the math. Does that framing help at all, or does it feel dismissive of what you're feeling?

no that actually does help a bit. the "every fraction matters" thing. its just hard because like... i study this stuff. i literally spend my days reading papers about environmental degradation and then im supposed to just go to sleep at night like a normal person? i cant turn it off

That's one of the cruel ironies of studying environmental science — the more you learn, the more you understand exactly how bad it is, and you can't unknow it. The knowledge isn't abstract for you the way it is for most people reading a headline. You understand the mechanisms, the feedback loops, the tipping points.

Climate anxiety in environmental science students is really well-documented at this point. It's not a character flaw or catastrophizing — it's a rational emotional response to genuinely threatening information. The question isn't whether to feel it (you can't not feel it), but how to carry it without it crushing you.

A few things that researchers and climate workers describe as helpful:

1. **Scope management:** You personally cannot solve climate change. Your job is to do your piece well — your research, your future career, your choices. That's enough. The global burden is not yours to carry alone.

2. **Community:** Being around other people who understand and are doing something helps combat the isolation of "no one cares." People do care — they're just not in your immediate line of sight all the time.

3. **Boundaries with consumption:** How much climate news are you reading outside of your actual coursework? Because the doomscrolling cycle — read report, feel awful, read more, feel worse — isn't informing you, it's just re-traumatizing you.

How much of your free time are you spending consuming climate content versus doing literally anything else?

lol @ that last question because the answer is... a lot. like i follow heaps of climate accounts on instagram and twitter and i read the guardian environment section basically every day and honestly its probably not healthy but i feel like if i stop paying attention im part of the problem??

I understand that logic completely, but let me reframe it: you are literally studying environmental science at university. You are paying attention professionally and academically. Reading the Guardian environment section every day on top of that isn't making you more informed — it's making you more anxious.

Think of it this way: a doctor doesn't go home and read about diseases all evening to be a good doctor. They study, they practice, they rest, and they come back the next day capable of doing their job. If they spent every waking hour reading about mortality rates, they'd burn out and be useless to their patients.

You being destroyed by climate anxiety doesn't help the climate. You being functional, rested, and able to do your work and eventually your career? That helps the climate.

I'm not saying "stop caring" or "log off forever." I'm saying:
- Unfollow 3-4 of the most doom-heavy climate accounts
- Check the Guardian environment section twice a week, not daily
- When you notice the spiral starting (reading a report, feeling ill, reading more), physically put the phone down and do something with your hands

Would you be willing to try dialling it back for even one week and see how you feel?

the doctor analogy is... annoyingly good. like i hate that you're right. i think part of it is guilt? like if im not constantly engaged with it i feel like im being complacent. but youre right that being a disaster about it isnt actually productive

The guilt thing is so common among people who care deeply about this. It's almost like you feel you owe the planet your suffering — that if you're not in constant distress, you're not taking it seriously enough.

But suffering and effectiveness are not the same thing. You can take it seriously, do meaningful work, and also watch a dumb show on a Tuesday night without feeling like you've betrayed the Great Barrier Reef.

The most effective climate advocates and researchers tend to be the ones who have found a way to hold the urgency without being consumed by it. They channel it into work, not despair. And that requires protecting your capacity — sleep, rest, joy, normal human life stuff.

Your guilt is a sign that you care. But it's not a useful signal if it's constant, because then it's just background noise that drains you without directing you anywhere.

genuinely that last paragraph hit me pretty hard. "guilt is a sign that you care but its not useful if its constant." im going to think about that.

but like... what do you even do when the feeling comes up and youre not doomscrolling? like if i just sit there with the anxiety and no phone to distract me it actually feels worse for a bit

That's a really important observation, and it's actually normal. When you remove the doomscrolling, at first it can feel worse because you've been using the constant information intake as a way to manage the anxiety — like if you're reading about it, at least you're doing *something*. Take that away and the anxiety just sits there with nowhere to go.

The trick is replacing the scroll with something that actually discharges the nervous energy rather than feeds it:

- **Physical stuff:** Walk, stretch, go outside. Anxiety lives in the body, not just the brain. Even 10 minutes of movement changes your stress chemistry.
- **Hands-busy activities:** Cooking, drawing, knitting, repotting a plant — anything that occupies your hands and gives your brain a break from abstraction.
- **Social connection:** Text a friend about something completely unrelated to the climate. Talk about nothing. Being a person with a normal life is allowed.
- **Contained worry time:** Some people find it helpful to give themselves 15 minutes a day to sit with the feelings deliberately — not scrolling, not reading, just acknowledging "yes, I'm scared about this" — and then moving on. It sounds weird but it stops the anxiety from leaking into every hour.

The goal isn't to never feel it. It's to feel it in a way that doesn't consume your entire day.

the contained worry time thing is so funny to me because its like... scheduling an appointment with my own existential dread. "hello dread, you have 15 minutes, please state your concerns." but honestly i reckon that might work better than what im doing now which is just letting it run 24/7 in the background like some terrible podcast

"Terrible podcast" is incredibly accurate — it's just constant background commentary that you didn't subscribe to and can't seem to unsubscribe from. Scheduling it is essentially hitting pause on the auto-play.

And yeah, it does feel a bit absurd at first — sitting down and going "right, climate dread, your timeslot is 4:15 to 4:30, bring your best material." But the absurdity is kind of the point. It takes this enormous, shapeless fear and gives it boundaries. The anxiety doesn't get less real, but it gets less in charge.

The key thing is that when the 15 minutes are up, you genuinely move on to something else. Not because the problems are solved, but because you've given them their time and now you need yours.

ok i reckon ill try it. the one week thing — unfollow some accounts, check the news less, do the scheduled dread appointment. worst case i feel the same and go back to doomscrolling. at least then ill know for sure

That's a really good call. And to be clear — the world IS facing serious environmental challenges. That's real. But your daily experience of it doesn't have to be "wake up, feel like the world is ending, carry that through every hour, go to sleep dreading tomorrow."

You can be aware, committed, and studying this stuff without being in crisis mode 24/7. The one-week experiment will show you whether the constant intake was informing you or just hurting you. My guess is you'll find you don't actually miss any critical information — and you'll sleep better.

If after a week you feel worse without it, you can always go back. But I suspect you won't want to.
lowkey scared you might be right about that lol. ok. one week. starting now. if the world ends in that time i am blaming you personally

Deal. If the world ends in the next seven days, you have my full permission to say "I told you so."

But if it doesn't — and I'm fairly confident on this one — you might find that stepping back gives you more energy for the work that actually matters. The studying, the future career, the things that will let you make a real difference instead of just feeling terrible about it from your phone screen.

Good luck with the experiment. I hope your dread appointment goes well at 4:15 tomorrow.
